refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; log all link transitions; calculate availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time; compare against 0.999 threshold. Acceptance requires independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate safe state transition within defined timeout.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics to the tele-operator and Safety Manager.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Redundant network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; dual-modem architecture; geographic diversity of base stations.
DP-B: Single high-reliability network link with enhanced error correction and retransmission; single-modem with adaptive modulation.
DP-C: Hybrid approach: primary link via licensed spectrum (low latency, high reliability) + secondary link via unlicensed spectrum (fallback); dual-modem with intelligent switching logic.
DP-D: Mesh network topology with multi-hop relay capability; distributed base stations; self-healing routing.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid dual-link architecture with primary licensed-spectrum link (optimized for low-latency command/telemetry) and secondary unlicensed-spectrum fallback link; dual-modem hardware with intelligent link-selection logic; automatic failover on primary link degradation; continuous link quality monitoring and reporting.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation link availability to network infrastructure (base station coverage, spectrum licensing, ISP SLA). This is acceptable because the FR explicitly requires customer-defined availability—the customer must commit to network infrastructure support.
DP-C couples link quality monitoring to Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface). This is acceptable and necessary: link degradation must trigger safety alerts.
DP-C does not couple to vehicle propulsion, steering, or payload systems—link availability is independent of vehicle mechanics.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is a decoupled design: primary and secondary links operate independently; failure of one does not degrade the other. Failover logic is stateless and deterministic. Acceptable coupling to external infrastructure (network) and to Safety Services (required for safety).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will provide or guarantee access to licensed-spectrum base station coverage at the worksite.
ASSUMPTION: Unlicensed-spectrum (e.g., 4G/5G or private LTE) is available as fallback at the worksite.

RISK: If both links fail simultaneously (e.g., site-wide network outage), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: define acceptable downtime budget; require customer to validate network redundancy.
RISK: Dual-modem hardware increases cost and power consumption. Mitigation: cost-benefit analysis required; power budget must be validated against vehicle battery capacity.
RISK: Intelligent failover logic introduces software complexity and potential bugs. Mitigation: rigorous testing of failover scenarios; formal verification of state machine.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound but requires explicit definition of: (1) failover timeout (how long primary link must be degraded before secondary is activated), (2) link quality thresholds (signal strength, latency, packet loss) that trigger failover, (3) rollback logic (when does secondary revert to primary), (4) customer network infrastructure requirements (coverage maps, SLA commitments). These should be captured in a separate DP refinement or in the IM specification.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the dual-modem hardware, link-selection logic, failover state machine, link quality monitoring, and all interfaces to the Vehicle (Tasking uplink) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert downlink). The module does NOT own the physical network infrastructure (base stations, spectrum licensing) or the tele-operator console UI (owned by external stakeholder).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands (steering, throttle, payload control) to Vehicle via Tasking interface. Requires low-latency, high-reliability delivery. Failover logic ensures Tasking is only sent when link is confirmed operational.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, system health) from Vehicle via Telemetry interface. Failover logic detects Telemetry loss and triggers link-down state.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends link quality alerts and link-down notifications to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. Enables Safety Manager to monitor link health and initiate safe shutdown if needed.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces to external network infrastructure (base stations, ISP) via Network Link. This is a logical interface representing the dual-modem hardware and its connection to primary and secondary networks. Not owned by Teleoperation module but required for operation.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system. Assigning DP-C to NGHE Solution would diffuse link availability responsibility across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Not recommended; too coarse-grained.

Vehicle module owns propulsion, steering, payload, and onboard sensors. Link availability (modem hardware, failover logic) is not a vehicle subsystem concern. Assigning DP-C to Vehicle would couple link management to vehicle mechanics. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of dual-modem hardware, link-selection logic, failover state machine, and link quality monitoring. All DP-C elements (primary link, secondary link, intelligent switching, monitoring) are contained within Teleoperation scope. Highly cohesive. Recommended.

Safety Services owns hazard detection, risk assessment, and safety alerts. Link availability monitoring is a prerequisite for safety but not a Safety Services responsibility. Assigning DP-C to Safety Services would blur the boundary between link management and safety logic. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-C (hybrid dual-link architecture). Rationale: (1) Teleoperation owns the modem hardware and link-selection logic; (2) Teleoperation is responsible for maintaining the Tasking and Telemetry interfaces; (3) Failover logic is a Teleoperation concern, not a Vehicle or Safety Services concern; (4) Link quality monitoring feeds SafetyAlert but is not a Safety Services responsibility. Containment score 0.95 indicates high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module depends on external Network Link interface (base stations, ISP). If network infrastructure is unavailable, Teleoperation cannot achieve 0.999 availability. Mitigation: customer must validate network infrastructure; acceptance criteria must include network SLA commitment.
RISK: Dual-modem hardware increases Teleoperation module complexity and cost. Mitigation: detailed design review required; cost-benefit analysis; power budget validation.

RISK: Failover logic introduces state machine complexity. Potential for race conditions or deadlocks if not carefully designed. Mitigation: formal verification of failover state machine; extensive testing of edge cases (simultaneous link loss, link flapping, etc.).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation module assignment is sound. Recommend: (1) Define detailed interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (latency, throughput, reliability requirements); (2) Specify failover timeout and link quality thresholds; (3) Allocate power budget for dual-modem hardware; (4) Require customer to provide network infrastructure SLA; (5) Plan for formal verification of failover state machine.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid dual-link architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999). DP-C provides redundancy (primary + secondary links) and intelligent failover, which are the primary mechanisms to achieve high availability. Assumptions about network infrastructure are explicit and acceptable. No FR-DP mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on external Network Link interface (base stations, ISP). This is acceptable because the FR explicitly requires customer-defined availability—the customer must commit to network infrastructure.
Failover logic couples Teleoperation to Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface. This is necessary and acceptable: link degradation must trigger safety alerts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score for DP-C. All dual-modem hardware, link-selection logic, failover state machine, and link quality monitoring are contained within Teleoperation scope. No diffusion to Vehicle or Safety Services. Ownership boundary is clear.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable via continuous link state monitoring during evaluation period. Teleoperation module can log all link transitions and calculate availability = (total_time - downtime) / total_time. Acceptance requires independent verification by Safety Manager and Site Manager. Feasible.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Teleoperation module is the clear owner of link availability. Responsibilities are: (1) maintain dual-modem hardware, (2) implement failover logic, (3) monitor link quality, (4) report link status via SafetyAlert. Allocation is clear and unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Define detailed interface contracts for Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, and Network Link (latency, throughput, reliability, timeout requirements).
Specify failover timeout (e.g., 100 ms) and link quality thresholds (e.g., signal strength > -100 dBm, latency < 500 ms, packet loss < 1%) that trigger failover.
Allocate power budget for dual-modem hardware; validate against vehicle battery capacity.
Require customer to provide network infrastructure SLA (coverage maps, uptime commitments, redundancy guarantees).
Plan for formal verification of failover state machine (e.g., model checking, theorem proving).
Define safe state transition logic: what happens when both links fail? (e.g., vehicle enters autonomous safe mode, operator receives alert, vehicle stops within safe distance).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic. The refined FR has identified four sub-FRs: (1) establish command uplink, (2) establish telemetry downlink, (3) detect link loss and initiate safe state, (4) provide link quality metrics. Each sub-FR requires further decomposition to identify specific DPs and IMs. DP-C (hybrid dual-link architecture) is sound at this level but must be decomposed into sub-DPs for each sub-FR. Recommend proceeding to next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command uplink from tele-operator to vehicle with latency ≤ 500 ms and reliability ≥ 99.9%.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry downlink from vehicle to tele-operator with latency ≤ 1000 ms and reliability ≥ 99.9%.

FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss (both primary and secondary links down) and initiate safe state transition (vehicle autonomous safe mode, operator alert) within 2 seconds.

FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link quality metrics (signal strength, latency, packet loss, link state) to the tele-operator console and Safety Manager dashboard with update frequency ≥ 1 Hz.

assumptions: Customer will provide or guarantee access to licensed-spectrum base station coverage at the worksite.
Unlicensed-spectrum (e.g., 4G/5G or private LTE) is available as fallback at the worksite.
Evaluation period is at least 30 days to achieve statistically significant availability measurement.
Vehicle battery capacity is sufficient to power dual-modem hardware (estimated 20-30 W continuous).
Tele-operator console and Safety Manager dashboard are external to NGHE Solution and will be provided by customer or integrator.
Network infrastructure (base stations, ISP) is not owned by NGHE Solution but is a prerequisite for operation.
open_risks: RISK: If both links fail simultaneously (e.g., site-wide network outage), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: define acceptable downtime budget; require customer to validate network redundancy.
RISK: Dual-modem hardware increases cost and power consumption. Mitigation: cost-benefit analysis required; power budget must be validated against vehicle battery capacity.
RISK: Intelligent failover logic introduces software complexity and potential bugs. Mitigation: rigorous testing of failover scenarios; formal verification of state machine.

RISK: Link flapping (rapid primary-secondary-primary transitions) could cause operator confusion or safety issues. Mitigation: implement hysteresis in failover logic (e.g., require primary link to be degraded for 5 seconds before switching to secondary).

RISK: Latency on secondary link may be higher than primary, affecting operator control responsiveness. Mitigation: specify maximum acceptable latency for secondary link; test operator control performance with secondary link.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure SLA from customer (coverage maps, uptime commitments, redundancy guarantees).
Dual-modem hardware specifications (power consumption, size, weight, cost, supported frequencies).
Failover logic design document (state machine, timeout values, link quality thresholds, hysteresis parameters).
Power budget analysis (total vehicle power consumption with dual-modem hardware).
Formal verification report for failover state machine (model checking or theorem proving results).
Test results for failover scenarios (primary link loss, secondary link loss, simultaneous loss, link flapping, latency variation).
Operator control performance evaluation with secondary link (latency, responsiveness, safety impact).
Field trial data from evaluation period (link availability, downtime events, root cause analysis).
